claim: The Maha Yuga of 4,320,000 years is derived from 12,000 divine years × 360 human years per divine year.
The 4:3:2:1 ratio for yuga lengths originates from Vedic dice game terminology where Krita=4, Treta=3, Dvapara=2, Kali=1.
The Surya Siddhanta uses 4,320,000 years as the fundamental cycle because it produces integer planetary revolution counts.
The Sun completes 4,320,000 revolutions per Maha Yuga in the Surya Siddhanta.
The sidereal year in the Surya Siddhanta is 365.25636 days, matching modern values.
En-men-lu-ana of Bad-tibira ruled 43,200 years according to the Sumerian King List.
The antediluvian total in Berossus is 432,000 years (120 sars).
The Great Pyramid's height × 43,200 approximates Earth's polar radius.
Sri Yukteswar argued the 432,000-year Kali Yuga is a multiplication error and proposed 24,000-year cycles.
Aryabhata proposed equal yugas of 1,080,000 years each.
Brahmagupta criticized Aryabhata's equal-yuga system in Brahmasphutasiddhanta chapter 11.
The precession of the equinoxes is ~25,920 years (ancient value) or ~25,772 (modern). 25,920/60 = 432.
Sexagesimal (base-60) originated with Sumerians c. 3100 BCE.
The Yuga Purana describes 10,000 divine years per mahayuga, differing from the later 12,000.
Al-Beruni recorded the yuga system in Kitab al-Hind around 1030 CE.
The Saptarshi calendar uses a 2,700-year cycle based on precession.
David Pingree documented transmission of Babylonian/Greek astronomy to India.
The number 43,200 appears as seconds in 12 hours of modern time measurement.
The Manusmriti describes yuga lengths with the 4:3:2:1 ratio in divine years.
The critical assessment of Hancock's pyramid theory notes measurements only work in modern meters.
